input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware consists of lower and upper level chassis and autonomy elements (perception, planning, and actuation modules). The vehicle operates in the automotive domain to demonstrate Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and is constrained by on‑board ECU resources, making it suitable for off‑loading computation to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface streams real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and enables users to view, save, and generate reports of ACC‑relevant data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed calculation) is executed on the DT (cloud) and the computed desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation, relieving the on‑board ECU; (3) Remote Management – the UI allows operators and developers to enable/disable ACC, set the desired speed, and issue emergency‑braking commands over low‑latency communication while respecting safety‑critical constraints.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference architecture: a Data Collection and Device Control Entity gathers sensor data, a Digital Twin Entity hosts the off‑loaded ACC logic, and a User Entity provides the UI. Functional Entities handle data transformation, actuation, and user interaction. OPC Unified Architecture can be used for data exchange. Cloud computing resources host the ACC computation. The prototype source code is publicly available at https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin and a demonstration video at https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between the physical vehicle and its digital twin with an accuracy of 100 ms, which is the limit imposed by the hardware; safety‑critical vehicular standards typically demand tighter (<100 ms) synchronization, but the implementation settles on 100 ms. This synchronization supports the low end‑to‑end latency needed for ACC computation off‑loading and remote management, implicitly targeting sub‑100 ms round‑trip communication.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 entity model and consists of three entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on separate hardware resources (e.g., on‑board ECU, edge or cloud compute). This yields a decentralized layout where each entity hosts its own functional entities implemented as ROS2 nodes, communicating via ROS2 topics/services. There is a single digital‑twin instance per physical vehicle; no multiple concurrent twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration is built on ROS2 middleware: each functional entity is packaged as a ROS2 package containing one or more nodes, and entities exchange data via ROS2 publish/subscribe and service mechanisms. The Physical Twin Entity handles sensor pre‑processing, actuation, and device communication; the Digital Twin Entity provides data management, control logic, and the off‑loaded ACC computation; the User Entity supplies 3D visualization and a control‑panel UI. Functional entities are mapped to appropriate compute nodes (on‑vehicle ECU versus cloud) to manage resources and coordinate actions across the system.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Inter‑entity communication uses ROS2 as the primary data‑exchange mechanism. For integration with external systems, the design references ISO 23247, which permits the use of standards such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA); thus the DTS can interoperate with enterprise systems or other digital twins via OPC UA gateways, although the current implementation relies on ROS2 communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DTS incorporates a behavioral ACC model that determines the lead vehicle, computes a desired speed, and returns that speed to the physical twin for actuation. Perception is supported by a point‑cloud clustering and tracking algorithm that processes 3‑D sensor data to generate detected object tracks and a projected driving path. Data sources include real‑time vehicle state variables (e.g., speed, position) and 3‑D point‑clouds obtained from the vehicle's advanced sensors. The Digital Twin Entity hosts data‑management functions and a Data Storage component (not aligned to a specific functional entity) that enables viewing, saving, and report generation through the UI. Model relationships are such that the perception pipeline (clustering & tracking) supplies the lead‑vehicle identification used by the ACC behavioral model, whose output (desired speed) drives the actuation on the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation on the physical twin is performed by the vehicle’s throttle and braking systems, which receive commanded desired speed values from the Digital Twin. Remote control commands transmitted via the UI include enable/disable ACC, setting a target speed, and issuing an emergency braking command. These commands are executed on the Physical Twin Entity, which hosts the actuation functionality. Safety‑critical operation imposes a low‑latency requirement (≈100 ms synchronization accuracy) for command transmission and execution.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor suite (LiDAR, motor, steering servo, vehicle state) streams raw data to the DTS. The raw LiDAR point cloud is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (Data Pre‑processing FE) which converts the 2‑D point list into object tracks. All data streams then pass through the Data Translation FE (ISO 23247 Data Translation) where they are serialized, message types aligned, and unit conversions applied. Within the DTS the components exchange messages over a ROS 2 middleware network. Communication between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity (or User Entity) crosses the vehicle boundary via a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The processed vehicle‑state, object tracks and projected driving path are fed to the 3‑D Visualization FE for real‑time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Commands generated by the User Interface FE (Control Panel sub‑component) are transmitted to the Physical Twin Entity’s Actuation FE. Supported command types include enable/disable of ACC, setting a desired speed, and emergency braking. Commands travel over the same ROS 2 intra‑system messages and Wi‑Fi/UDP inter‑entity channel. The Actuation FE forwards the validated command to the vehicle’s motor and steering servos for execution. The system provides feedback by reporting the updated vehicle state back through the Data Translation FE to the UI, confirming command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a hybrid network architecture. Inside each entity, software components are connected through ROS 2 (DDS‑based) middleware, enabling publish/subscribe communication among FE packages. Between entities (Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin ↔ User Entity) the transport is a Wi‑Fi link using UDP packets. The design requires low end‑to‑end latency to satisfy real‑time ACC monitoring and remote management; clock synchronization across devices is kept within 100 ms accuracy. No explicit bandwidth, redundancy, or security protocol specifications are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Computation offloading of the ACC algorithm is performed by deploying the ACC service on a remote platform, termed the Digital Twin, which may be cloud‑based or edge hardware. Each functional element is packaged as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes, allowing the services to be started on the hosting platform. Storage of collected data is handled by a Data Storage component (without a directly mapped FE). Scalability is implied by the modular ROS 2 deployment, but concrete scalability mechanisms, storage capacities, or availability guarantees are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. First, key software components are identified (Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators are defined (Fig. 2). The components are then mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) and each FE is realized as an individual ROS 2 package; a package may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the required functionality. The resulting three entities are deployed on separate hardware: the Physical Twin Entity hosts data pre‑processing, actuation and device‑communication FEs; the Digital Twin Entity contains data‑management and control FEs; the User Entity hosts the User Interface FE, which is split into a 3‑D visualization component and a control‑panel component. Development was performed by two graduate students as a course project, and a public demonstration video is provided. No explicit QA, testing, or version‑control procedures are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built to comply with ISO 23247 (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing), using its reference architecture to organise entities and functional entities. While ISO 23247 does not prescribe concrete protocols, the implementation leverages OPC UA (IEC 62541) for the Data Translation FE and adopts ROS 2 as the middleware. Safety considerations follow ISO 26262, and the authors propose promoting safety to a first‑class citizen. The architecture is also aligned with automotive software standards such as AUTOSAR, enabling hardware‑agnostic component definition and potential integration with existing vehicle platforms. The approach emphasizes independence from specific data formats and protocols, facilitating interoperability across domains.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study adopts safety‑critical engineering practices from ISO 26262‑1:2018, explicitly promoting functional‑safety activities (hazard analysis, risk assessment, and safety‑critical verification) to a first‑class concern in the automotive digital twin architecture. The ISO 23247 reference architecture is recognised as insufficient for safety‑critical use, leading to the integration of ISO 26262 processes for the twin’s safety assurance. Security‑related measures are referenced through the inclusion of blockchain‑based e/e‑traceability (Heber et al., 2017) to provide immutable logging of model and data exchanges, thereby supporting integrity and tamper detection. Additionally, the V2X service requirements specified in 3GPP TS 22.185 (Release 15) are cited, implying the use of the standard’s defined security mechanisms (e.g., authentication, confidentiality, and integrity protection) for vehicle‑to‑everything communications within the twin’s connected environment.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components

components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations

1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)


(UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data
Translation FE, or implement a custom communication layer.
Although the standard was developed for the manufacturing
domain, its RA is an appealing feature for DT developers in
other domains. Shtofenmakher and Shao [16] adapt the RA
of ISO 23247 to space debris detection. In their approach,
the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four
distinct FEs, each providing a specific viewpoint for space
data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.
Both types of OMEs are able to provide data through the
Data Collection and Control Entity. Cederbladh et al. [17]
investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness
of the ISO 23247 RA for automotive systems.


_B. Automotive digital twins_
